output.title: Landlord Withholding Security Deposit? 5 Steps to Get It Back (No Lawyer Needed)
output.meta_description: Landlord withholding your security deposit? Learn your tenant rights, illegal deductions, and how to send a demand letter in 60 seconds for $19.99.
content_outline: The Silent Treatment: Why Your Landlord Is Withholding Your Security Deposit
Know the Law: Is Your Landlord Required to Return Your Deposit and When?
Can a Landlord Keep Your Deposit? Debunking Illegal Deductions and ‘Cleaning Fees’
What to Do When Your Landlord Won’t Give Your Deposit Back: A 3-Step Action Plan
Security Deposit Dispute Letter 101: What to Say (and What to Avoid)
Viral TikTok & Reel Hooks: Social Scripts for Frustrated Tenants Ready to Share Their Story
The 60-Second Solution: How Deposits-Back.com Puts Your Money Back in Your Pocket
key_points: State laws set strict deadlines—usually 14 to 30 days—for landlords to return a deposit or provide an itemized deduction list; missing this deadline often weakens their legal standing.
Normal wear and tear, routine carpet aging, and pre-existing damage cannot be deducted; only documented damage caused by the tenant beyond ordinary use is a valid claim.
A formal security deposit dispute letter is the fastest, cheapest leverage before small claims court, and many landlords comply immediately upon receiving legal-looking documentation.
TikTok / Reel Hook 1: POV: It’s day 31 and your landlord just texted ‘processing.’ Here’s the state law they’re breaking. #TenantRights #SecurityDeposit
TikTok / Reel Hook 2: I got my $2,400 deposit back with one letter—no lawyer, no sign-up, $19.99. Here’s the exact hack. #Renting #MoneyTips
TikTok / Reel Hook 3: ‘You can’t deduct painting from my deposit.’ The four words that saved me $800. Watch till the end. #Landlord #DepositBack
output.cta: Stop chasing your landlord. Generate a legally sound security deposit demand letter at Deposits-Back.com for just $19.99—in 60 seconds, no sign-up, no subscription.
output.target_audience: Frustrated US renters who have moved out and are dealing with a landlord withholding, delaying, or wrongfully deducting their security deposit.
